did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with the EfficientNet-B3 architecture, employing transfer learning and fine-tuning, to classify five types of facial skin diseases (acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea) from image data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a Convolutional Neural Network (EfficientNet-B3) for classifying facial skin diseases. It details specific methodology including dataset collection from Kaggle (14,950 images), preprocessing, transfer learning, fine-tuning, and reports original results such as 96.61% testing accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

title: KLASIFIKASI PENYAKIT KULIT WAJAH MENGGUNAKAN CONVOLUTIONAL NEURAL NETWORK EFFICIENTNET-B3
journal: JITET (Jurnal Informatika dan Teknik Elektro Terapan)
brief_summary: This study developed a Convolutional Neural Network using the EfficientNet-B3 architecture to classify five types of facial skin diseases: acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea. The model was trained on a public Kaggle dataset comprising 14,950 augmented images, utilizing transfer learning and fine-tuning techniques. Evaluation on the test set demonstrated a testing accuracy of 96.61%, with average precision, recall, and F1-score values of 0.97. The confusion matrix indicated high classification performance with minimal errors between classes, particularly for visually similar conditions. The results suggest that this approach effectively improves detection accuracy and can support medical personnel in early diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Five discrete diagnostic categories of facial skin diseases: acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea.
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification using a Convolutional Neural Network (EfficientNet-B3) with transfer learning and fine-tuning. The model predicts discrete disease labels from image data. Evaluation metrics include testing accuracy (96.61%), precision, recall, and F1-score (0.97), along with a confusion matrix to assess classification performance between classes.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Facial Skin Disease Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset obtained from Kaggle containing images of facial skin diseases. The dataset was downloaded as a structured folder based on disease classes without label modification.

use.training: Used for training the EfficientNet-B3 model via transfer learning and fine-tuning, with data augmentation applied to increase variation and reduce overfitting.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process to monitor accuracy and loss convergence.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of the model's performance, achieving 96.61% testing accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as testing accuracy (96.61%), precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix.

EfficientNet-B3 (CNN with Transfer Learn.notes: The model achieved high classification performance with minimal errors between classes. Confusion matrix indicated most predictions were on the main diagonal. Errors were relatively small and occurred mainly between visually similar classes (e.g., Acne and Rosacea, Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma). No significant overfitting was observed.

Kaggle Facial Skin Disease Dataset.notes: The dataset consisted of 14,950 augmented images across five classes: acne (2,992), actinic keratosis (2,987), basal cell carcinoma (2,991), eczema (2,986), and rosacea (2,994). Data was split 80% for training and 20% for validation/testing (equal split). Fine-tuning began at epoch 60, leading to significant performance improvement. Validation accuracy consistently exceeded 0.95.

conclusions: The Convolutional Neural Network using the EfficientNet-B3 architecture with transfer learning and fine-tuning effectively classifies five types of facial skin diseases (acne, actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, eczema, and rosacea) with high performance.
The model achieved a testing accuracy of 96.61%, an overall accuracy of 97%, and average precision, recall, and F1-score values of 0.97.
Fine-tuning starting at epoch 60 resulted in significant performance improvements, demonstrating the importance of adapting pre-trained weights to specific medical image characteristics.
The model demonstrated strong generalization capabilities with no significant overfitting, evidenced by stable training and validation curves and low validation loss (0.129).
Classification errors were minimal and primarily occurred between visually similar classes, such as Acne and Rosacea, and Actinic Keratosis and Basal Cell Carcinoma.
The EfficientNet-B3 model outperformed the ResNet-50 architecture (which achieved 94% accuracy in referenced studies), likely due to more optimal fine-tuning and balanced class distribution.
The approach has the potential to support medical personnel in the early diagnosis of facial skin diseases, particularly in areas with limited dermatologist access.
important_remarks: The study relies entirely on a single public Kaggle dataset without external validation, limiting the assessment of generalizability to diverse clinical populations or imaging devices.
Data augmentation techniques (rotation, flipping, scaling, intensity adjustment) were applied during training, which may artificially inflate performance metrics compared to raw clinical images.

The dataset distribution is artificially balanced (~2,990 images per class), which does not reflect the real-world prevalence of facial skin diseases and may mask performance issues on rare conditions.
No statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported solely as descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score).
The paper does not report specific hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, batch size) or loss function details, hindering reproducibility.
Potential data leakage risk exists as the dataset was downloaded directly from Kaggle without explicit verification of train/test split integrity or patient-level separation.
Clinical relevance is limited to a proof-of-concept for early diagnosis support; the paper does not validate the model's impact on actual clinical decision-making or patient outcomes.

Comparison with ResNet-50 is based on referenced literature (94% accuracy) rather than a direct side-by-side experimental comparison in this study, making the performance claim relative rather than absolute.
